article_meta.title: Politics Here Are The Top 10 Lies Of Liz Cheney And The January 6th Committee By: Tristan Justice January 06, 2025 8 min

reasoning: The model does a reasonably good job identifying genuine bias patterns in the author's own prose. It correctly avoids flagging quoted text from sources like Mike Lee, Loudermilk, Hutchinson, etc. The bias instances flagged are largely defensible - the article is heavily opinionated editorial content from The Federalist with extensive author-level bias. However, there are some issues: (1) the bias type labels don't always match the predefined list (e.g., 'Loaded Language', 'Minimization', 'One-sided Reporting/Source Bias' are not in the defined list), and (2) some flagged items could be more precisely categorized. The model catches many genuine instances and doesn't commit attribution rule violations.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: The model correctly avoids flagging any quoted text. All flagged instances come from the author's own prose - 'disgraced', 'Soviet-style inquisition', 'hysterical claims', 'textbook case of projection', etc. are all author language. No attribution rule violations.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Most flagged items are defensible instances of author bias. The article is clearly an opinion/editorial piece with heavy authorial voice throughout. A couple items are slightly borderline - e.g., the Carlson item describes the author's framing choices which is fair but a bit of a stretch. Overall most are genuine.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model catches many key instances but could have flagged additional ones like 'Capitol demonstrations' (minimizing euphemism in the headline/lede), 'conspiracy peddled by the Jan. 6 Committee', and the overall structural slant of presenting all 10 items as definitively 'lies'. Some additional instances of Mind Reading could have been caught.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: Multiple labels used are not from the predefined list: 'Loaded Language', 'Loaded Language/Labeling', 'Minimization', 'One-sided Reporting/Source Bias', 'Loaded Language/Mockery', 'False Balance/Minimization', 'Editorializing/Loaded Language', 'Unsubstantiated Generalization'. These should map to defined types like Word Choice, Subjective Qualifying Adjectives, Slant, Spin, Mudslinging/Ad Hominem, Opinion Statements Presented as Fact, etc. This is a significant labeling issue.
